summary: Atwood revisits his earlier criticism of pure object-oriented programming, citing Paul Graham's argument that OOP generates busywork disguised as productivity. Graham compares OOP enthusiasts to programmers who padded fanfold pages with elaborate comments, noting that simple operations become entire files of classes and methods. Atwood advocates for moderation, arguing that a little object orientation goes a long way and that pushing everything into objects creates maintenance nightmares. His guiding principle is simplicity, measured by producing the least volume of source code.
key_points: Pure object-oriented programming is oversold and can generate false productivity
OOP allows programmers to incorporate scaffolding directly into source code, inflating its apparent complexity
Simple operations that could be one-liners become entire files of classes and methods in OOP-heavy codebases
Moderation in OOP usage prevents 'giant masses of repetitive code'
The best approach typically produces the least volume of source code
money_quotes: Object-oriented programming generates a lot of what looks like work.
Object-oriented programming is like crack for these people: it lets you incorporate all this scaffolding right into your source code.
Something that a Lisp hacker might handle by pushing a symbol onto a list becomes a whole file of classes and methods.
It is a good tool if you want to convince yourself, or someone else, that you are doing a lot of work.
A little object orientation goes a long way.
I like to err on the side of simplicity, and that typically means the approach that produces the least volume of source code.
key_insight: Object-oriented programming can become a tool for generating the appearance of productivity rather than actual simplicity, and moderation in its use leads to more maintainable code.
